title: Spanning-tree-modulus certificate (v4)
description: Certificate format for PR 2 (untrusted builder) / PR 5 (Lean verifier), per Certification_Plan.md §6. A flat, ORDERED list of 'pieces' -- one per within-round deflation core, or per solver round if it needed no further deflation -- glued by a single left-fold of Pmf.glue (lean/DiscreteModulusCert/Glue.lean). Order matters: it must match discovery order (round 1's pieces, in their own discovery order, then round 2's, ...), the same order FactoredPmf.pieces / SolverTrace.rounds already produce. Invariants this schema does NOT and cannot check (the verifier's actual job, not JSON-Schema's): every piece's 'edges' disjoint from every earlier piece's; the union of all pieces' 'edges' equals the whole top-level 'graph.edges' list; every declared tree is a genuine forest (Multigraph.IsForest, lean/DiscreteModulusCert/ForestDecide.lean) that is maximal within its piece; each piece's tree weights are non-negative and sum to exactly 1. No 'eta'/'rho' fields: the verifier always derives both from 'pieces' (compositionally, from each piece's own local marginal -- see the plan doc's note on why the glued top-level pmf's marginal must never be computed directly, since Pmf.glue's support is a literal Cartesian product and is exponential in the number of pieces). There is nothing for the certificate to assert about eta/rho that the verifier doesn't already have to (re)compute itself, so there is nothing to gain by shipping a value that would only ever be trusted, not checked. Rational numbers are always [numerator, denominator] pairs of JSON integers -- NEVER floating point, and NEVER assume 53-bit-safe integer range: numerators/denominators are arbitrary precision (they come from exact Fraction arithmetic on the Python side), so a JSON parser that silently coerces big integers to double-precision floats will silently corrupt a certificate. denominator is always a positive integer; fractions need not be in lowest terms.

certificate_version.description: Independent from the PR 1 solver-trace's own "version" field -- they evolve on different schedules.

num_vertices.description: Vertices are the integers 0..num_vertices-1; no separate vertex-label list, matching Boost's vecS vertex_descriptor.

edges.description: edges[i] gives global edge i's two endpoint vertices. Array position IS the edge's stable global index -- every 'edges' list elsewhere in this document indexes into this array, never relabeled. Parallel edges (repeated [u, v] pairs) are legal and distinct edge identities.

pieces.description: One entry per block in the flat laminar-family fold (lean/DiscreteModulusCert/Glue.lean's PieceList), in discovery order.

rational.description: [numerator, denominator]; see the top-level description for why these are never floats.

edgeIndex.description: An index into the top-level graph.edges array -- always global, never piece-local.

vertexLabel.description: An original-graph vertex index (0..num_vertices-1), or an opaque synthetic label for a vertex created by deflation-contracting an earlier piece (e.g. "__core_0__", matching pmf_construction.py's own synthetic naming). Informational only -- see 'vertices' below.

edges.description: A spanning tree of this piece: global edge indices (a subset of the enclosing piece's own 'edges').
This piece's own edge scope, as global indices. Must be disjoint from every earlier piece's 'edges' (verifier-checked, not schema-checked).

vertices.description: INFORMATIONAL ONLY, not load-bearing for verification -- every check the verifier performs runs purely on edge sets (Pmf.glue, IsForest, etc. never mention vertices). Kept for traceability back to solver_trace.hpp's own per-round 'vertices' field and for human debugging only; a verifier is not required to parse or cross-check this field against anything.

trees.description: This piece's local pmf: a Finset of edge sets plus a rational weight each (DiscreteModulusCert.Pmf's exact shape). Weights must be non-negative and sum to exactly 1 (verifier-checked).
